title: Kristan Hawkins’ Flawed Reasoning vs Scripture
author: Pate Anglin
orientation: Pate Anglin critiques Students for Life of America president Kristan Hawkins for grounding her case for incremental, compromised abortion legislation in pragmatic outcomes rather than Scripture. He contrasts a Pro-Life syllogism ("save as many lives as possible") with an Abolitionist syllogism rooted in obedience to God's commands against partiality, unequal weights, and unjust decrees.
audiences: Pro-Life advocate weighing incrementalism vs. abolition
Christian voter wrestling with biblical standards for civil law
Students for Life member curious about the SFA challenge
Pastor or church leader teaching on civil ethics

questions.question: What is the core problem Anglin says Kristan Hawkins and the Pro-Life Movement share?
questions.audience: Pro-Life advocate weighing incrementalism vs. abolition
questions.answer: Anglin argues both Hawkins and the broader Pro-Life Movement appeal to a manmade pragmatic standard rather than Scripture when defending compromised legislation, leaving them unable to justify their moral charges of "sin" or "pride" against abolitionists.
questions.quote: Kristan Hawkins has a standard problem, and so does the broader Pro-Life Movement.

questions.question: What specific scriptural objection did the SFA officer raise against Hawkins's heartbeat bill?
questions.audience: Christian voter wrestling with biblical standards for civil law
questions.answer: Jeremiah, the Students for Abolition officer, argued that Hawkins's legislative approach violates God's explicit commands against unequal weights and measures by appealing to Proverbs 20:23 and 11:1.
questions.quote: a Students for Abolition (SFA) officer challenged Hawkins—founder and president of SFLA—arguing that her legislative approach violates God’s explicit commands against “unequal weights and measures” (Proverbs 20:23; 11:1).

questions.question: How does Anglin formalize the Pro-Life Movement's reasoning, and what does he say is wrong with it?
questions.audience: Pastor or church leader teaching on civil ethics
questions.answer: He frames it as a syllogism prioritizing saving as many lives as possible through incremental laws, then runs a reductio ad absurdum: if maximizing lives saved is the only standard, even immoral acts like bombing clinics could be justified by their outcomes—which Pro-Lifers themselves rightly reject.
questions.quote: If the standard is only “saving as many lives as possible,” then any means, no matter how immoral, could be justified by its outcomes. For instance, if bombing an abortion clinic delayed or deterred abortions, would that make it right? Of course not!

questions.question: Why does Anglin call incremental laws an act of partiality?
questions.audience: Christian voter wrestling with biblical standards for civil law
questions.answer: Because Scripture defines partiality as applying the law unequally—excusing one class while condemning another—and incremental bills do exactly that by sorting victims and perpetrators by gender or circumstance instead of moral truth.
questions.quote: Biblically, partiality means applying the law unequally, excusing one class of sinners while condemning another (Leviticus 19:15; Proverbs 17:15). Incremental laws commit this very injustice by distinguishing between victims and perpetrators based on gender or circumstance rather than moral truth.

questions.question: What three functions of civil law does Anglin say the Pro-Life Movement misunderstands?
questions.audience: Pastor or church leader teaching on civil ethics
questions.answer: He identifies instruction (teaching right and wrong), restraint (deterring evil), and justice (executing judgment) as God's designed functions of civil law, all rooted in cited passages from Galatians, Romans, Deuteronomy, Ecclesiastes, Proverbs, and Amos.
questions.quote:
Instruction – It teaches what is right and wrong (Gal. 3:24; Rom. 3:20; Deut. 11:18–21).  
2. **Restraint** – It deters and restrains evil (Deut. 13:11; Rom. 13:1–4; Eccl. 8:11).  
3. **Justice** – It executes justice (Prov. 21:15; Amos 5:24).

questions.question: How do incremental laws affect a mother's view of repentance, according to Anglin?
questions.audience: Pastor or church leader teaching on civil ethics
questions.answer: By framing mothers as victims rather than moral agents, incremental laws give a repentant mother nothing to repent of—the wrongdoing is portrayed as something done to her, not by her.
questions.quote: Even a repentant mother would find, under such laws, nothing for which to repent, since the wrongdoing is framed as something done *to* her, not *by* her.

questions.question: What did the Students for Life regional director's answer reveal about the movement's true governing principle?
questions.audience: Students for Life member curious about the SFA challenge
questions.answer: When pressed on why SFLA opposes equal-protection bills that would save more lives, the director appealed to mothers being victims of abortion, exposing that a feminist "second-victim" premise—not maximizing lives saved—is the actual governing principle.
questions.quote: Her response exposed the movement’s true governing principle: not the unqualified goal of saving lives, but submission to a feminist premise that casts mothers as the “second victims” of abortion.

questions.question: What evidence does Anglin offer that abolitionist legislative efforts are bearing fruit?
questions.answer: He points to 23% of Georgia House Republicans supporting HB 441, an Equal Protection bill advancing out of committee in Louisiana, and similar efforts emerging nationwide as signs that faithfulness to God's standard is producing political gains.
questions.quote: In Georgia, 23% of House Republicans supported HB 441; in Louisiana, an Equal Protection bill advanced out of committee; and similar efforts are emerging nationwide.

questions.question: How does Anglin push back on Pro-Life claims of legislative success?
questions.audience: Pro-Life advocate weighing incrementalism vs. abolition
questions.answer: He notes that compromised bills preserve legal exemptions for self-managed abortions that go unreported, that documented self-managed abortions have risen even in "Total" ban states, and that citing rising birth rates as proof of lives saved is unreliable—birth rates also surged during COVID lockdowns.
questions.quote: Reputable sources have documented increases in self-managed abortions in states with “Total” bans... When hard data is lacking, Pro-Lifers cite rising birth rates as proof of success, but birth rates are not a one-to-one measure of lives saved. By that logic, will they also take credit for the surge in births during the COVID-19 lockdowns?

questions.question: What is the abolitionist syllogism Anglin offers as a contrast?
questions.answer: It begins with man's chief end being to glorify God, holds that God forbids partiality and unjust decrees on child sacrifice, observes that incremental Pro-Life laws show partiality, and concludes such laws are unjust, unbiblical, and immoral disobedience to God.
questions.quote:
**Premise 1:** The chief end of man is to glorify God and enjoy Him forever.  
**Premise 2:** God forbids partiality, unequal weights and measures, and unjust decrees concerning child sacrifice.  
**Premise 3:** Incremental Pro-Life laws show partiality (protecting some while excusing others).  
**Conclusion 1:** Therefore, such laws are unjust and unbiblical.

questions.question: What does Anglin say is the ultimate question behind every legislative argument?
questions.audience: Christian voter wrestling with biblical standards for civil law
questions.answer: He insists the decisive question is "By what standard?"—and he rejects worldly-wise pragmatism, the view that the ends justify the means, in favor of obedience as the Christian standard.
questions.quote: Ultimately, every moral and legislative argument returns to one question: *By what standard?* The Pro-Life establishment answers with worldly-wise pragmatism, the ever-shifting measure of what seems to “work” or “save the most.” God’s people cannot be worldly-wise pragmatists.

related.title: A Mother Is a Magistrate: Why Duress Is No Defense for Abortion
related.reason: Directly engages the same equal-protection vs. incrementalism debate, refuting the second-victim/duress framing Anglin critiques here.

related.title: Abolitionism Spreading to Campuses Across the Nation
related.reason: Also by Pate Anglin and rooted in the Students for Abolition campus context that frames the Hawkins confrontation.

related.title: Abolitionist, Not Pro-Life
related.reason: Foundational Abolitionists Rising page articulating the same Pro-Life vs. Abolitionist standard distinction Anglin develops.

related.title: Students For Life Advocates Legal Do-It-Yourself Abortions
related.reason: Earlier critique of Students for Life specifically on the same legal-immunity-for-mothers position Anglin attacks.

related.title: Five Reasons Abolitionists Oppose Incremental Pro-Life Bills
related.reason: Provides the broader catalog of abolitionist objections to incremental legislation that this article condenses into a syllogism.

related.title: Incremental Laws (of All Sorts) Undermine Repentance
related.reason: Develops in depth the repentance argument Anglin makes about mothers having nothing to repent of under incremental laws.

related.title: The Holiness of God Destroys Pro-Life Incrementalism
related.reason: Parallel theological argument that God's character itself is the standard against which incremental Pro-Life strategy fails.
